---
title: "The Likelihood: Reading Probability Backwards"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # data / observed points
CURVE  = "#c1121f"     # the likelihood / log-likelihood curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the peak / true value
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded densities
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This opens the **Likelihood Spine** — the engine room
> of the whole track. It assumes [INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*], which
> installed the one idea everything here rests on: that a statistic computed from data is
> itself a random variable with a **sampling distribution**. From the Probability
> Foundations track it borrows, each built there from the ground up, the **probability
> mass and density functions** of the Bernoulli/binomial, Poisson, and Normal families
> ([PROB-10], [PROB-12], [PROB-13]) and the fact that the **joint density of an
> independent sample is the product of the individual densities** ([PROB-08]). Nothing
> else is assumed above the Calculus-I floor — every derivative and logarithm identity is
> on the page. This article installs the central *object* of estimation: the
> **likelihood**, which reads the model's probability formula *backwards* — fixing the data
> and letting the parameter vary. It sets up [INFR-B2] (maximum likelihood *maximizes* this
> object), [INFR-B3] (Fisher information is its *curvature*), and [INFR-E4] (the Bayesian
> posterior is this object *times a prior*).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A probability model is a formula $p(x \mid \theta)$ that, given a parameter $\theta$,
tells you how probable each possible dataset $x$ is. Inference needs the reverse: the data
$x$ are fixed and known — you already collected them — and the parameter $\theta$ is the
unknown. The **likelihood** is that same formula read backwards: $L(\theta) = p(x \mid
\theta)$ with $x$ held fixed and $\theta$ allowed to vary. This single change of viewpoint
— which symbol is the variable — is the foundation of nearly all of estimation. This
article builds the likelihood from first principles, proves carefully that it is **not** a
probability distribution over $\theta$ (it need not integrate to one, and re-labeling the
parameter changes its height), derives why we always work with its **logarithm** (products
become sums, and the peak does not move), writes down the log-likelihood end-to-end for the
four canonical models — Bernoulli/binomial, Normal with known and with unknown variance,
and Poisson — and introduces the **score function** $\partial_\theta \log L$, the slope
whose root the next article will chase. The single claim the article defends is that *the
likelihood is the bridge from a forward-facing probability model to a backward-facing
statement about the unknown that generated the data*.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A probability model gives you $p(x \mid \theta)$: with the parameter $\theta$ **fixed**,
this is a distribution over the **data** $x$ — it says how the world rolls the dice. The
**likelihood** is the *same function* with the roles swapped: the data $x$ you actually
observed are **fixed**, and $\theta$ is the **variable**,
$$
L(\theta) \;=\; p(x \mid \theta), \qquad x \text{ fixed.}
$$
For an independent sample $x_1,\dots,x_n$, the joint probability factors into a product,
so the likelihood is
$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n p(x_i \mid \theta),
\qquad\text{and we almost always use its logarithm}\qquad
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \log p(x_i \mid \theta).
$$
The likelihood **ranks** parameter values by how well each explains the data you saw — a
larger $L(\theta)$ means "$\theta$ makes these data more probable." It is **not** a
probability distribution over $\theta$: it need not integrate to one, and it carries no
prior belief. It is a *function we will maximize*, and its slope, the **score**
$\partial_\theta \ell(\theta)$, points the way to the top.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The hinge is the quiet phrase *roles swapped*. A coder
meets $p(x \mid \theta)$ as a function you *call* with a fixed $\theta$ to get
probabilities of outcomes — `binom.pmf(k, n, p)` with `p` pinned. The likelihood is the
exact same expression, but now `k` is pinned (it is the count you observed) and `p` is the
free variable you sweep. Same arithmetic, opposite question. The forward question is *"given
this coin, how surprising is 7 heads?"* The backward question — the inference question — is
*"given that I saw 7 heads, which coins are plausible?"* The likelihood is the function that
answers the second by re-reading the first.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the idea {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **how do we measure how well a particular
parameter value explains the data we actually saw?** Every definition and derivation below
answers a piece of that. If a step feels like notation for its own sake, return here.

## The problem: a model points the wrong way

By [INFR-A1] we have estimators — recipes that turn a sample into a number — but so far we
have invented them by instinct. "Estimate the mean? Average the data." That works for the
mean because the recipe is obvious. It is hopeless the moment the question is sharper:
*estimate the rate of a Poisson process from a week of counts; estimate the bias of a coin
from a streak of flips; estimate both the center and the spread of a Normal at once.* We
need a **principle** — a single rule that, handed any probability model and any dataset,
*manufactures* an estimator instead of asking us to guess one. The likelihood is the raw
material that principle (maximum likelihood, [INFR-B2]) will be built from.

The obstacle is a mismatch of direction. A probability model is written **forwards**: you
fix the parameter $\theta$ — the true coin bias, the true Poisson rate — and the model
tells you the probability of each dataset the world might hand you. That is the natural way
to *specify* a model and the only way to *simulate* from it. But the inference situation is
backwards. You are not handed $\theta$ and asked for the data; you are handed the **data**
and asked for $\theta$. The data are the known, fixed, already-collected thing. The
parameter is the unknown. The model and the question point in opposite directions, and
something must turn the model around.

## The turn: fix the data, free the parameter

The turn is almost embarrassingly simple, and that simplicity is exactly why it took a
genius to *name* it and insist it be taken seriously. R. A. Fisher, in his 1922 paper *On
the Mathematical Foundations of Theoretical Statistics*, drew a permanent line between two
readings of one formula. The probability formula $p(x \mid \theta)$ has two slots. Decide
which slot is the variable:

- Pin $\theta$, let $x$ vary $\;\Rightarrow\;$ a **probability distribution** over datasets.
  This is what the model is *for*.
- Pin $x$ (at the data you saw), let $\theta$ vary $\;\Rightarrow\;$ the **likelihood**, a
  function of the parameter. This is what *inference* needs.

Fisher's word for the second reading was *likelihood*, and he was emphatic — almost
combative — that it is **not** a probability. The same symbols, the same arithmetic, but a
different object with different rules, because a different slot is now the variable. Holding
that distinction straight is the entire conceptual content of this article; the rest is
working it out for the models you will actually meet.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between probability and likelihood? People use the words
interchangeably."* In casual speech, yes; in statistics they are opposite readings of the
same function $p(x\mid\theta)$. **Probability** fixes the parameter and varies the data:
"given a fair coin, the probability of 7 heads in 10 flips is $0.117$." **Likelihood**
fixes the data and varies the parameter: "given that I observed 7 heads in 10 flips, the
likelihood of the bias $p=0.5$ is $0.117$, of $p=0.7$ is $0.267$, of $p=0.9$ is $0.057$."
The number $0.117$ is identical in both sentences — it is the *same evaluation of the same
formula* — but in the first it is one entry in a distribution over outcomes that sums to
one, and in the second it is one height on a curve over $p$ that does **not** sum (or
integrate) to one. The interviewer wants to hear that the distinction is *which argument is
held fixed*, and that likelihood is a function of the parameter, not a probability of it.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, here is the picture. You flipped a coin $10$ times and saw $7$ heads.
You do not know the coin's bias $p$. Walk a candidate $p$ from $0$ to $1$ and, at each
stop, ask the *forward* question the model can answer: *if the bias really were this $p$,
how probable would my exact observation — 7 heads in 10 — have been?* That probability,
plotted against $p$, is the likelihood curve. @fig-intuition draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-intuition
#| fig-cap: "The likelihood for a coin, having observed 7 heads in 10 flips. Each point asks the forward question 'if the bias were this $p$, how probable is exactly 7 heads in 10?' and plots that probability against $p$. The curve **ranks** candidate biases: $p=0.7$ (green) makes the data most probable, $p=0.5$ is plausible, $p=0.1$ is nearly ruled out. The curve is the same binomial formula read backwards — data fixed, parameter swept. It is a function of $p$, not a probability distribution over $p$: the shaded area under it is not one."
from math import comb

n, k = 10, 7
p_grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)
lik = comb(n, k) * p_grid**k * (1 - p_grid)**(n - k)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.fill_between(p_grid, lik, color=FILL, alpha=0.45)
ax.plot(p_grid, lik, color=CURVE, label=r"$L(p) = \binom{10}{7}\,p^7(1-p)^3$")
phat = k / n
ax.axvline(phat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, ls="--",
           label=fr"peak at $p = {phat:.1f}$")
for pv in (0.1, 0.5, 0.9):
    Lv = comb(n, k) * pv**k * (1 - pv)**(n - k)
    ax.scatter([pv], [Lv], color=DATA, zorder=4, s=40)
    ax.annotate(f"$p={pv}$", (pv, Lv), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(6, 6), fontsize=9, color=DATA)
ax.set_xlabel("candidate bias $p$")
ax.set_ylabel("likelihood $L(p)$")
ax.set_title("Likelihood ranks the candidate biases")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Three things to read off the picture, each of which the formal section makes precise:

- **The curve ranks, it does not measure probability of $\theta$.** Its *height* at $p$ is
  the probability of the **data** under that $p$, not the probability that $p$ is the truth.
  The whole curve is a comparison device: it says $p=0.7$ explains the data better than
  $p=0.5$, which beats $p=0.1$ by a lot.
- **The peak is the most-explanatory parameter.** The bias that makes the observed data
  most probable is $\hat p = 0.7$ — which happens to be $7/10$, the obvious estimate. That
  "happens to be" is no accident; it is the content of the next article.
- **The area under it is meaningless.** Nothing forces $\int_0^1 L(p)\,dp = 1$. The
  likelihood is a height-ranking, not a distribution. We prove this below, because it is the
  single most common misunderstanding of the object.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The likelihood, defined {#sec-defn}

We start with the forward object, the thing a probability model hands you, and then turn it
around. Throughout, $\theta$ denotes the unknown parameter (possibly a vector, e.g.
$\theta=(\mu,\sigma^2)$ for the Normal); the data are observations $x_1,\dots,x_n$.

For a single observation, the model supplies a **probability mass function** $p(x\mid\theta)$
when $x$ is discrete (it returns an actual probability, $P(X=x)$ under parameter $\theta$),
or a **probability density function** $f(x\mid\theta)$ when $x$ is continuous (its integral
over a region gives a probability). Both are *forward* objects: with $\theta$ fixed they
are non-negative and sum (or integrate) to one over all $x$. We write $p(\cdot)$ for either
and say "mass/density" when the distinction matters.

::: {#def-likelihood}
**Definition (likelihood).** Given observed data $x = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$ from a model with
parameter $\theta$, the **likelihood function** is the joint mass/density of the data,
*read as a function of $\theta$ with $x$ held fixed*:
$$
L(\theta) \;=\; L(\theta \mid x) \;=\; p(x \mid \theta).
$$
If the observations are **independent** (the i.i.d. case from [PROB-08]), the joint
mass/density factors into a product, so
$$
L(\theta) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n p(x_i \mid \theta).
$$
:::

The factorization is not new mathematics — it is the definition of independence imported
from probability, where for independent events the joint probability is the product of the
individual ones, and for independent continuous variables the joint density is the product
of the marginals ([PROB-08]). We state it here because *the product structure is the reason
logarithms enter*, which is the whole of @sec-loglik.

Two notational points, settled now so they never trip you later. First, the data $x$ are
written on the right of the bar as a reminder that they are fixed, but they are *not* a
conditioning event in the probability sense — the likelihood is a function of $\theta$
alone, and $x$ is a fixed parameter of that function. Many texts write $L(\theta)$ and drop
the $x$ entirely; we keep it when emphasis helps. Second, the likelihood is defined only up
to a positive multiplicative constant that does not involve $\theta$: if
$L(\theta) = c \cdot g(\theta)$ with $c>0$ free of $\theta$, then $L$ and $g$ rank
parameters identically and either may be called "the likelihood." This is why, in
@fig-intuition, the binomial coefficient $\binom{10}{7}$ — a constant in $p$ — can be kept
or dropped without changing where the peak sits or how the values compare. We will use this
freedom constantly.

## Why the likelihood is *not* a probability distribution over $\theta$ {#sec-not-prob}

This is the claim the whole article must defend, so we prove it rather than assert it. Two
independent reasons, each fatal on its own.

**Reason 1 — it need not integrate to one.** A probability density over $\theta$ would have
to satisfy $\int L(\theta)\,d\theta = 1$. The likelihood carries no such guarantee, because
the integration that *is* guaranteed runs over the wrong variable. The model's normalization
says: with $\theta$ fixed, summing/integrating over all **data** gives one,
$$
\sum_{x} p(x \mid \theta) = 1 \quad(\text{discrete}),
\qquad
\int p(x \mid \theta)\,dx = 1 \quad(\text{continuous}),
\qquad\text{for every fixed } \theta.
$$
Nothing in the model constrains the integral over $\theta$ with $x$ fixed. Take the coin of
@fig-intuition, dropping the constant $\binom{10}{7}$ (allowed, by the previous section).
Then $L(p) = p^7(1-p)^3$, and we can integrate it exactly. We do not need to *assume* any
"Beta integral" formula — a short integration by parts builds it from scratch. Write
$I(a,b) = \int_0^1 p^a(1-p)^b\,dp$. Integrate by parts with $u=(1-p)^b$ (so
$du = -b(1-p)^{b-1}\,dp$) and $dv = p^a\,dp$ (so $v = \tfrac{p^{a+1}}{a+1}$); the boundary
term $uv\big|_0^1$ vanishes because $p^{a+1}=0$ at $p=0$ and $(1-p)^b=0$ at $p=1$ (for
$b\ge 1$), leaving
$$
I(a,b) = -\int_0^1 v\,du
= \frac{b}{a+1}\int_0^1 p^{a+1}(1-p)^{b-1}\,dp
= \frac{b}{a+1}\,I(a+1,\,b-1).
$$
This **reduction** lowers the second exponent by one each time, at the cost of a factor
$\tfrac{b}{a+1}$ (then $\tfrac{b-1}{a+2}$, and so on). Applying it $b$ times drives the
second exponent to zero, where the integral is elementary:
$I(a+b,0)=\int_0^1 p^{a+b}\,dp = \tfrac{1}{a+b+1}$. Multiplying the collected factors,
$$
I(a,b) = \frac{b}{a+1}\cdot\frac{b-1}{a+2}\cdots\frac{1}{a+b}\cdot\frac{1}{a+b+1}
= \frac{a!\,b!}{(a+b+1)!},
$$
where the last equality just rewrites the descending products as factorials
($b(b-1)\cdots1 = b!$ in the numerator, and $(a+1)(a+2)\cdots(a+b+1) = \tfrac{(a+b+1)!}{a!}$
in the denominator). For our coin, $a=7$, $b=3$:
$$
\int_0^1 p^7(1-p)^3\,dp = \frac{7!\,3!}{(7+3+1)!} = \frac{5040 \cdot 6}{39\,916\,800}
= \frac{30\,240}{39\,916\,800} \approx 0.000757 \neq 1.
$$

```{python}
#| label: fig-not-prob
#| fig-cap: 'The likelihood is not a distribution over $\theta$. **Left:** with the parameter $p=0.7$ fixed, the binomial mass over the *data* $k$ sums to exactly one (blue bars) — that is what the model normalizes. **Right:** with the data $k=7$ fixed, the likelihood over the *parameter* $p$ integrates to $\approx 0.00076$, nowhere near one. Same formula, two readings: only the forward reading is a probability distribution.'
from math import comb

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))

# Left: forward reading, p fixed, distribution over data k.
p_fixed = 0.7
ks = np.arange(0, 11)
pmf = np.array([comb(10, int(k)) * p_fixed**k * (1 - p_fixed)**(10 - k) for k in ks])
axL.bar(ks, pmf, color=DATA, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_xlabel("data: number of heads $k$")
axL.set_ylabel("$p(k \\mid p=0.7)$")
axL.set_title(f"Forward: sums to {pmf.sum():.3f}")

# Right: backward reading, k=7 fixed, likelihood over p (constant dropped).
p_grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 600)
Lp = p_grid**7 * (1 - p_grid)**3
area = np.trapezoid(Lp, p_grid)
axR.fill_between(p_grid, Lp, color=FILL, alpha=0.5)
axR.plot(p_grid, Lp, color=CURVE)
axR.set_xlabel("parameter: bias $p$")
axR.set_ylabel("$L(p) = p^7(1-p)^3$")
axR.set_title(f"Backward: integrates to {area:.5f}")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"forward sum over data      = {pmf.sum():.6f}  (= 1, the model normalizes here)")
print(f"backward integral over p   = {area:.6f}  (not 1: likelihood is no distribution)")
```

**Reason 2 — re-labeling the parameter changes its height.** A genuine probability density
transforms under a change of variable with a Jacobian factor, precisely so that
probabilities (areas) are preserved. The likelihood does **no such thing** — it is just a
height, evaluated at whatever name you give the parameter. Concretely, suppose we
reparameterize the coin by its log-odds $\eta = \log\frac{p}{1-p}$ instead of $p$. The
likelihood of a particular *coin* is the same physical quantity however we label it, so we
simply substitute:
$$
L^*(\eta) = L\bigl(p(\eta)\bigr), \qquad p(\eta) = \frac{e^\eta}{1+e^\eta},
$$
with **no Jacobian factor** $\bigl|\frac{dp}{d\eta}\bigr|$. A probability density would
*require* that factor, $f^*(\eta) = f\bigl(p(\eta)\bigr)\bigl|\frac{dp}{d\eta}\bigr|$, to
keep total area at one. The likelihood refuses it, because it is not protecting any area.
This is exactly the property that makes the maximum-likelihood estimate **invariant** to
reparameterization (proved in [INFR-B2]): the peak of $L^*(\eta)$ sits at the log-odds of
the peak of $L(p)$, untouched by any stretching. We show both readings side by side in
@fig-reparam.

```{python}
#| label: fig-reparam
#| fig-cap: 'A genuine density needs a Jacobian under reparameterization; the likelihood does not. The likelihood in $p$ (left) is *substituted* into the log-odds $\eta$ (right) with no Jacobian — the peak simply moves to $\eta=\log\frac{0.7}{0.3}$ (green), and the height at the peak is unchanged. A probability density (grey dashed, right) would be reshaped by the factor $|dp/d\eta|$ to preserve area, bending it away from the bare likelihood. The likelihood protects no area, so it ignores the Jacobian.'
p_grid = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 600)
Lp = p_grid**7 * (1 - p_grid)**3

eta_grid = np.log(p_grid / (1 - p_grid))
# Bare likelihood substituted into eta: no Jacobian.
L_eta = Lp.copy()
# What a *density* would do: multiply by |dp/deta| = p(1-p).
dens_eta = Lp * (p_grid * (1 - p_grid))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(p_grid, Lp, color=CURVE)
axL.axvline(0.7, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5)
axL.set_xlabel("bias $p$"); axL.set_ylabel("$L(p)$")
axL.set_title("Likelihood in $p$")

axR.plot(eta_grid, L_eta, color=CURVE, label="likelihood (no Jacobian)")
axR.plot(eta_grid, dens_eta, color=GREY, ls="--",
         label=r"a density (with $|dp/d\eta|$)")
axR.axvline(np.log(0.7 / 0.3), color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5,
            label=r"peak $\eta=\log\frac{0.7}{0.3}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"log-odds $\eta=\log\frac{p}{1-p}$")
axR.set_ylabel("value")
axR.set_xlim(-4, 4)
axR.set_title("Re-expressed in $\\eta$")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not read the likelihood as "the probability that $\theta$ takes this value."** It is
not. $L(p=0.7)=0.267$ does **not** mean there is a $26.7\%$ chance the coin's bias is $0.7$.
It means: *if the bias were $0.7$, the data you saw would have probability $0.267$.* The
likelihood is a statement about the **data given a parameter**, organized as a function of
the parameter — never a probability statement about the parameter. The only framework in
which "$P(\theta = 0.7)$" is even a meaningful phrase is the Bayesian one, and getting there
requires multiplying the likelihood by a **prior** and re-normalizing — exactly Bayes'
theorem, the subject of [INFR-E4]. Until you supply a prior, the likelihood alone says
nothing about the probability *of* $\theta$.
:::

## Why we work with the log-likelihood {#sec-loglik}

Two forces push us, every single time, to take the logarithm of the likelihood before doing
anything with it. We derive both, because "always use logs" is folklore until you see why.

::: {#def-loglik}
**Definition (log-likelihood).** The **log-likelihood** is the natural logarithm of the
likelihood,
$$
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \log L(\theta).
$$
For an independent sample, applying $\log$ to the product in @def-likelihood and using
$\log(ab)=\log a+\log b$ turns it into a sum:
$$
\ell(\theta) \;=\; \log \prod_{i=1}^n p(x_i\mid\theta)
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \log p(x_i\mid\theta).
$$
:::

**Force 1 — products become sums, which we can differentiate and which add up
information.** A product of $n$ factors is a nightmare to differentiate: the product rule
forces every term to drag along all the others. A sum is trivial — the derivative of a sum
is the sum of the derivatives, term by term, with nothing entangled. Since the next article
finds the peak by differentiating, we *want* a sum. There is a deeper payoff too: because
$\ell(\theta) = \sum_i \log p(x_i\mid\theta)$ is an **additive** accumulation over the
data, each independent observation contributes its own additive piece. That additivity is
what makes "twice the data carries twice the information" literally true later ([INFR-B3]),
and it is why the score and the Fisher information will both be *sums over observations*.

**The logarithm does not move the peak.** This is the fact that makes the swap legitimate,
and it has to be proved, not waved at. The natural logarithm is **strictly increasing**: if
$0 < a < b$ then $\log a < \log b$. (Reason from Calc 1: its derivative is $\frac{d}{dt}\log
t = 1/t > 0$ for all $t>0$, and a function with everywhere-positive derivative is strictly
increasing.) A strictly increasing transform preserves *order*: it can stretch and squash
the vertical axis but never reorders which input is largest. So if $\theta^\star$ maximizes
$L$ — meaning $L(\theta^\star)\ge L(\theta)$ for all $\theta$ — then applying the
order-preserving $\log$ to both sides gives $\ell(\theta^\star) = \log L(\theta^\star) \ge
\log L(\theta) = \ell(\theta)$ for all $\theta$, so the very same $\theta^\star$ maximizes
$\ell$. The maximizer is identical; only the height is rescaled. @fig-loglik shows the two
curves sharing a peak.

```{python}
#| label: fig-loglik
#| fig-cap: 'The log never moves the peak. **Left:** the likelihood $L(p)=p^7(1-p)^3$ for 7 heads in 10. **Right:** its log-likelihood $\ell(p)=7\log p + 3\log(1-p)$. The vertical scales differ wildly — $\ell$ is large and negative — but both peak at exactly $p=0.7$ (green line). Because $\log$ is strictly increasing it preserves the *location* of the maximum while turning the awkward product into a clean sum.'
p_grid = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 600)
Lp = p_grid**7 * (1 - p_grid)**3
ell = 7 * np.log(p_grid) + 3 * np.log(1 - p_grid)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(p_grid, Lp, color=CURVE)
axL.axvline(0.7, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5)
axL.set_xlabel("$p$"); axL.set_ylabel("$L(p)$")
axL.set_title("Likelihood")

axR.plot(p_grid, ell, color=CURVE)
axR.axvline(0.7, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label="peak at $p=0.7$")
axR.set_xlabel("$p$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\ell(p)=7\log p + 3\log(1-p)$")
axR.set_ylim(-12, -5)
axR.set_title("Log-likelihood (same peak)")
axR.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**Force 2 — numerical survival.** Even setting calculus aside, the raw likelihood is
unusable on a computer for any real sample. Each factor $p(x_i\mid\theta)$ is a probability
or density, often well below $1$; multiplying $n$ of them drives the product toward zero
exponentially fast, and floating-point hardware *underflows* — silently rounds the product
to exactly $0.0$ — long before $n$ is large. Once it is $0.0$, every comparison is a tie and
all information is destroyed. Logs convert the doomed product into a sum of moderate
negative numbers, which add up to a large but perfectly representable negative number. We
make the underflow concrete in @fig-underflow.

```{python}
#| label: fig-underflow
#| fig-cap: 'Why logs are not optional in practice. For a Bernoulli sample of $p=0.5$, the raw likelihood $\prod_i p(x_i)$ (left, log-scaled axis) collapses toward the smallest representable float and then underflows to exactly zero around $n\approx 1075$ — past that point the product is indistinguishable from impossible. The log-likelihood (right) declines linearly and stays a finite, well-behaved number for any sample size. Real datasets have thousands to millions of points; only the log survives.'
ns = np.arange(1, 1400, 1)
# Each Bernoulli(0.5) factor contributes 0.5 to the product.
raw = 0.5 ** ns                      # underflows to 0.0 for large n
logL = ns * np.log(0.5)              # stays finite and linear

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.semilogy(ns, np.where(raw > 0, raw, np.nan), color=CURVE)
underflow_n = ns[np.argmax(raw == 0.0)] if np.any(raw == 0.0) else None
if underflow_n is not None:
    axL.axvline(underflow_n, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4)
    axL.annotate(f"underflow\nat n$\\approx${underflow_n}",
                 (underflow_n, 1e-200), fontsize=8, color=GREY,
                 ha="right")
axL.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"raw $\prod_i p(x_i)$ (log axis)")
axL.set_title("Raw likelihood underflows to 0")

axR.plot(ns, logL, color=CURVE)
axR.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\ell = \sum_i \log p(x_i)$")
axR.set_title("Log-likelihood stays finite")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why do we maximize the log-likelihood instead of the likelihood — doesn't taking a log
change the answer?"* It changes the *value* but not the *location* of the maximum, and the
location is all we want. The proof is one line: $\log$ is strictly increasing (its
derivative $1/t$ is positive), so it preserves the ordering of values and therefore the
argmax. We switch to logs for three reasons you should be able to list: (1) it turns the
product $\prod_i p(x_i\mid\theta)$ into a sum $\sum_i \log p(x_i\mid\theta)$, which is what
makes differentiation tractable; (2) that sum is additive over independent observations,
which is the seed of "information adds" in Fisher-information theory; and (3) it averts
floating-point underflow, since a product of many sub-one factors collapses to zero while a
sum of logs stays finite. The follow-up — *"natural log or log base 10?"* — base does not
matter for the argmax (a positive constant multiple), but natural log is conventional
because $\frac{d}{dt}\log t = 1/t$ keeps the derivatives clean.
:::

## The canonical likelihoods, derived end-to-end {#sec-canonical}

We now write down $\ell(\theta)$ for the four models you will meet constantly. Each is a
direct application of @def-loglik to a mass/density imported from the Probability track —
the only work is honest algebra, every step shown.

### Bernoulli and binomial {#sec-bernoulli}

A **Bernoulli** trial is a single coin flip: $X=1$ ("head") with probability $p$, $X=0$
("tail") with probability $1-p$. The mass function can be written in one expression that
selects the right factor by the value of $x\in\{0,1\}$ (since $p^1(1-p)^0=p$ and
$p^0(1-p)^1=1-p$):
$$
p(x\mid p) = p^{x}(1-p)^{1-x}, \qquad x\in\{0,1\}.
$$
For $n$ independent flips $x_1,\dots,x_n$, the likelihood is the product, which we simplify
by collecting exponents (the exponents on $p$ add up, and so do those on $1-p$):
$$
L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^n p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}
     = p^{\sum_i x_i}\,(1-p)^{\sum_i (1-x_i)}
     = p^{\,k}\,(1-p)^{\,n-k},
\qquad k := \sum_{i=1}^n x_i,
$$
where $k$ is simply the total number of heads. Taking logs with $\log(a^c)=c\log a$:
$$
\boxed{\;\ell(p) = k\log p + (n-k)\log(1-p).\;}
$$ {#eq-loglik-bernoulli}
The **binomial** model is the same content packaged differently: if we observe only the
count $k$ out of $n$ trials (not the individual flips), its mass adds the counting factor
$\binom{n}{k}$ for the number of head/tail orderings that give $k$ heads:
$$
p(k\mid p) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}
\;\Rightarrow\;
\ell(p) = \log\binom{n}{k} + k\log p + (n-k)\log(1-p).
$$
The extra term $\log\binom{n}{k}$ is **constant in $p$**, so by the constant-freedom of
@sec-defn it does not affect the ranking of biases or the location of the peak — the
binomial and Bernoulli likelihoods are equivalent for inference about $p$. This is the curve
of @fig-intuition with $n=10$, $k=7$.

### Poisson {#sec-poisson}

The **Poisson** model counts events in a fixed window — calls to a help line in an hour,
mutations along a stretch of DNA, goals in a match — when events occur independently at an
average rate $\lambda>0$. Its mass over a count $x\in\{0,1,2,\dots\}$ is
$$
p(x\mid\lambda) = \frac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!}.
$$
For $n$ independent counts $x_1,\dots,x_n$, the likelihood is the product; we collect the
$\lambda$ powers (exponents add), the exponentials (exponents add, giving $e^{-n\lambda}$),
and the factorials:
$$
L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{\lambda^{x_i} e^{-\lambda}}{x_i!}
= \frac{\lambda^{\sum_i x_i}\, e^{-n\lambda}}{\prod_i x_i!}.
$$
Take logs, using $\log(ab)=\log a+\log b$, $\log(a/b)=\log a-\log b$, $\log(\lambda^s)=s\log
\lambda$, and $\log e^{-n\lambda} = -n\lambda$:
$$
\boxed{\;
\ell(\lambda) = \Bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i} x_i\Bigr)\log\lambda \;-\; n\lambda
\;-\; \underbrace{\textstyle\sum_i \log(x_i!)}_{\text{constant in }\lambda}.
\;}
$$ {#eq-loglik-poisson}
The final sum involves only the data, not $\lambda$, so it is an additive constant we may
keep or drop. What remains, $(\sum_i x_i)\log\lambda - n\lambda$, carries all the
dependence on the rate.

### Normal with known variance {#sec-normal-known}

The **Normal** (Gaussian) model with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ has density
$$
f(x\mid\mu,\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr).
$$
Suppose for now $\sigma^2$ is **known** and only the mean $\mu$ is unknown. For an
independent sample, the likelihood is the product of densities,
$$
L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)
= (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2\Bigr),
$$
where we collected the $n$ identical front factors into one power and used
$\prod_i e^{a_i} = e^{\sum_i a_i}$ to merge the exponentials. Taking logs (the log of the
front factor, plus the exponent itself since $\log e^z = z$):
$$
\boxed{\;
\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2)
\;-\; \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2.
\;}
$$ {#eq-loglik-normal-known}
Look at the structure: the first term is constant in $\mu$, and the second is a *negative*
multiple of the sum of squared deviations $\sum_i (x_i-\mu)^2$. So **maximizing this
log-likelihood over $\mu$ is identical to minimizing the sum of squared deviations** — the
likelihood viewpoint and the least-squares viewpoint coincide exactly under Gaussian noise.
This is the same equivalence the Regression track meets from the other side in
[REGR-01]; here we are seeing *why* it holds, at the level of the general principle, before
any line is fit.

### Normal with unknown variance — a two-parameter likelihood {#sec-normal-unknown}

Now the realistic case: **both** $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown, so $\theta=(\mu,\sigma^2)$ is
a two-dimensional parameter. The density is the same, but we no longer treat $\sigma^2$ as a
fixed constant — it is a variable we keep. Writing $v := \sigma^2$ to reduce clutter,
$$
L(\mu, v) = (2\pi v)^{-n/2}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2v}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2\Bigr),
$$
and the log-likelihood, keeping every term that now depends on a parameter:
$$
\boxed{\;
\ell(\mu, v) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi) \;-\; \frac{n}{2}\log v
\;-\; \frac{1}{2v}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2.
\;}
$$ {#eq-loglik-normal-unknown}
We split $\log(2\pi v) = \log(2\pi) + \log v$ so the $\log v$ term — which *does* depend on
the variance — stands on its own; only $-\frac n2\log(2\pi)$ is a genuine constant. This is
a **surface** over the $(\mu, v)$ plane, not a curve, and finding its peak in [INFR-B2]
will require a partial derivative in each direction — exactly the multivariable critical
point we previewed. @fig-normal-surface draws it for a small real-style sample.

```{python}
#| label: fig-normal-surface
#| fig-cap: 'The two-parameter Normal log-likelihood $\ell(\mu, \sigma^2)$ for a sample of $n=12$ measurements, drawn as a contour map over the $(\mu,\sigma^2)$ plane. Unlike the one-parameter coin, the likelihood is now a *surface*: every $(\mu,\sigma^2)$ pair gets a height. The single peak (green) sits at the sample mean and the sample-based variance. The next article finds this peak by setting both partial derivatives to zero — the multivariable version of ''flat at the top''.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
data = rng.normal(loc=20.0, scale=3.0, size=12)
xbar = data.mean()

mu_grid = np.linspace(xbar - 3, xbar + 3, 200)
v_grid = np.linspace(3.0, 22.0, 200)
MU, V = np.meshgrid(mu_grid, v_grid)
n = len(data)
SS = np.array([[np.sum((data - m) ** 2) for m in mu_grid] for _ in v_grid])
LL = -0.5 * n * np.log(2 * np.pi) - 0.5 * n * np.log(V) - SS / (2 * V)

mle_mu = xbar
mle_v = np.mean((data - xbar) ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
cs = ax.contour(MU, V, LL, levels=18, cmap="viridis")
ax.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=7, fmt="%.0f")
ax.scatter([mle_mu], [mle_v], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=5,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.5, label="peak")
ax.set_xlabel(r"mean $\mu$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"variance $\sigma^2$")
ax.set_title(r"Normal log-likelihood surface $\ell(\mu,\sigma^2)$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"sample mean (peak in mu)        = {mle_mu:.3f}")
print(f"mean squared dev (peak in var)  = {mle_v:.3f}")
```

## The score: the slope whose root we will chase {#sec-score}

We close the formal development by introducing the object that turns the likelihood from a
picture into a computation. The peak of $\ell$ — the most-explanatory parameter — is, by
ordinary Calc 1, a place where the curve is **flat**: its derivative is zero. That derivative
has a name.

::: {#def-score}
**Definition (score function).** The **score** is the derivative of the log-likelihood with
respect to the parameter,
$$
U(\theta) \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\,\ell(\theta)
\;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\sum_{i=1}^n \log p(x_i\mid\theta)
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log p(x_i\mid\theta).
$$
(The sum came outside the derivative because the derivative of a sum is the sum of the
derivatives — the additivity bought by the log in @sec-loglik.) When $\theta$ is a vector,
the score is the vector of partial derivatives, one per component.
:::

The score is the *slope* of the log-likelihood curve. Where the curve climbs, $U(\theta)>0$
— pushing the candidate parameter higher would explain the data better. Where it descends,
$U(\theta)<0$. At the peak the slope is zero: $U(\hat\theta)=0$. That equation,
$U(\theta)=0$, is the **score equation**, and solving it is exactly how the next article
finds the maximum-likelihood estimate. We compute the score for our running coin to make it
concrete. From @eq-loglik-bernoulli, $\ell(p) = k\log p + (n-k)\log(1-p)$, and using
$\frac{d}{dp}\log p = \frac1p$ and $\frac{d}{dp}\log(1-p) = \frac{-1}{1-p}$ (chain rule, the
inner derivative of $1-p$ being $-1$):
$$
U(p) = \frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{n-k}{1-p}.
$$
Setting $U(p)=0$ and solving (multiply through by $p(1-p)$, giving $k(1-p)=(n-k)p$, i.e.
$k - kp = np - kp$, i.e. $k = np$) yields $\hat p = k/n$ — the sample proportion, the
"obvious" estimate, now *derived* as the root of the score rather than guessed. We are
deliberately stopping one inch short of that conclusion, because turning the score equation
into the maximum-likelihood estimator — and checking it is a maximum, not a minimum or a
boundary — is the whole job of [INFR-B2]. @fig-score shows the log-likelihood and its
score sharing the crossing point.

```{python}
#| label: fig-score
#| fig-cap: 'The score is the slope of the log-likelihood. **Top:** $\ell(p)=k\log p+(n-k)\log(1-p)$ for 7 heads in 10, peaking at $p=0.7$. **Bottom:** the score $U(p)=\ell''(p)=k/p-(n-k)/(1-p)$, which is positive while $\ell$ climbs, negative while it falls, and crosses zero (green) exactly under the peak. Solving the *score equation* $U(p)=0$ recovers $\hat p = k/n = 0.7$ — the root we will chase in the next article.'
n, k = 10, 7
p_grid = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 600)
ell = k * np.log(p_grid) + (n - k) * np.log(1 - p_grid)
score = k / p_grid - (n - k) / (1 - p_grid)
phat = k / n

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6.4, 5.4), sharex=True)
axT.plot(p_grid, ell, color=CURVE)
axT.axvline(phat, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5)
axT.set_ylabel(r"$\ell(p)$")
axT.set_ylim(-12, -5)
axT.set_title("Log-likelihood and its score")

axB.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axB.plot(p_grid, score, color=CURVE)
axB.axvline(phat, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=fr"$U(p)=0$ at $p={phat:.1f}$")
axB.set_xlabel("$p$"); axB.set_ylabel(r"score $U(p)=\ell'(p)$")
axB.set_ylim(-60, 60)
axB.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the score function, and why do we care about where it is zero?"* The score is the
gradient of the log-likelihood, $U(\theta)=\partial_\theta\ell(\theta)$ — the slope of the
"how well does $\theta$ explain the data" curve. We care about its root because a smooth
function is flat at its peak, so the most-explanatory parameter (the MLE) satisfies the
*score equation* $U(\hat\theta)=0$. Two follow-ups worth pre-empting: the score has expected
value **zero** at the true parameter (a fact proved in [INFR-B3] that makes it a kind of
unbiased "estimating equation"), and its *variance* is the **Fisher information** — the
curvature of $\ell$ at the peak, which measures how sharply the data pin down $\theta$. So
the score is the hinge connecting this article (the likelihood object) to the next two (the
MLE as its root, and Fisher information as its spread).
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the construction on real, messy data — never a toy rigged to flatter the method.
We carry two examples end to end and then walk to the edge where the likelihood's
*comparison* nature, by itself, stops being enough.

## Example 1 — goals per match: a Poisson likelihood {#sec-ex-poisson}

Counts of goals scored by a team across a season are a textbook home for the Poisson model:
goals are rare-ish, roughly independent events accumulating at some average rate $\lambda$
per match. We take a season's worth of match goal-counts and build the likelihood over the
rate $\lambda$, by hand from @eq-loglik-poisson.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-poisson
#| fig-cap: 'A Poisson likelihood from a season of goal counts ($n=30$ matches). **Left:** the observed counts, with the data-determined rate $\bar x$ marked. **Right:** the log-likelihood $\ell(\lambda)=(\sum x_i)\log\lambda - n\lambda$ (constant dropped) over candidate rates, peaking exactly at $\lambda=\bar x$ (green). The curve ranks rates by how well each explains the whole season; a rate far from the sample mean — even one only a goal off — is sharply down-weighted.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
goals = rng.poisson(lam=1.6, size=30)
n = len(goals)
total = goals.sum()
xbar = goals.mean()

lam_grid = np.linspace(0.3, 4.0, 500)
# log-likelihood with the sum-of-log-factorials constant dropped
ell = total * np.log(lam_grid) - n * lam_grid

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
vals, counts = np.unique(goals, return_counts=True)
axL.bar(vals, counts, color=DATA, alpha=0.85)
axL.axvline(xbar, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=fr"$\bar x={xbar:.2f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("goals in a match"); axL.set_ylabel("number of matches")
axL.set_title("Observed counts"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(lam_grid, ell, color=CURVE)
axR.axvline(xbar, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=fr"peak at $\lambda=\bar x={xbar:.2f}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"rate $\lambda$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\ell(\lambda)$ (const. dropped)")
axR.set_title("Poisson log-likelihood"); axR.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"n matches       = {n}")
print(f"total goals     = {total}")
print(f"sample mean     = {xbar:.4f}")
```

Reading the arithmetic against @eq-loglik-poisson: with $\sum_i x_i = `{python} f"{total}"`$
goals over $n = `{python} f"{n}"`$ matches, the log-likelihood is $\ell(\lambda) =
`{python} f"{total}"`\log\lambda - `{python} f"{n}"`\lambda$ (dropping the data-only
constant). Its slope — the score — is $U(\lambda) = \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\lambda} - n =
\frac{`{python} f"{total}"`}{\lambda} - `{python} f"{n}"`$, which is zero when $\lambda =
\frac{\sum_i x_i}{n} = \bar x = `{python} f"{xbar:.3f}"`$. So the peak sits precisely at the
sample mean, and the figure confirms it. Notice that we did all of this *without ever
computing a probability of $\lambda$* — we only ranked rates by how probable each makes the
observed season. The likelihood is doing exactly its job: comparison, not belief.

## Example 2 — measurement error: a Normal likelihood, mean and variance together {#sec-ex-normal}

A laboratory weighs the same reference mass $n$ times; each reading is the true weight plus
independent Gaussian measurement noise. Both the center $\mu$ (the best estimate of the
weight) and the spread $\sigma^2$ (the instrument's noise level) are unknown, so this is the
two-parameter likelihood of @eq-loglik-normal-unknown. We profile the surface along each
axis to see how the data pin down each parameter.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-normal
#| fig-cap: 'Profiling the two-parameter Normal log-likelihood for $n=15$ repeated weighings. **Left:** holding $\sigma^2$ at its best value and sweeping $\mu$, the curve peaks at the sample mean $\bar x$ (green). **Right:** holding $\mu=\bar x$ and sweeping $\sigma^2$, the curve peaks at the mean squared deviation $\frac1n\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2$ (green). Each slice of the surface is itself a one-parameter likelihood, and its peak gives that parameter''s most-explanatory value.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
true_w = 50.0
reads = rng.normal(loc=true_w, scale=0.4, size=15)
n = len(reads)
xbar = reads.mean()
v_mle = np.mean((reads - xbar) ** 2)

def loglik(mu, v):
    return -0.5 * n * np.log(2 * np.pi) - 0.5 * n * np.log(v) \
        - np.sum((reads - mu) ** 2) / (2 * v)

mu_grid = np.linspace(xbar - 0.4, xbar + 0.4, 400)
ell_mu = np.array([loglik(m, v_mle) for m in mu_grid])

v_grid = np.linspace(0.04, 0.5, 400)
ell_v = np.array([loglik(xbar, v) for v in v_grid])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(mu_grid, ell_mu, color=CURVE)
axL.axvline(xbar, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=fr"$\bar x={xbar:.3f}$")
axL.set_xlabel(r"mean $\mu$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\ell(\mu,\hat\sigma^2)$")
axL.set_title(r"Slice along $\mu$"); axL.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(v_grid, ell_v, color=CURVE)
axR.axvline(v_mle, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=fr"$\hat\sigma^2={v_mle:.4f}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"variance $\sigma^2$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\ell(\bar x,\sigma^2)$")
axR.set_title(r"Slice along $\sigma^2$"); axR.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"sample mean              xbar     = {xbar:.4f}")
print(f"mean squared deviation   v_mle    = {v_mle:.4f}")
print(f"(for comparison) n-1 sample var   = {np.var(reads, ddof=1):.4f}")
```

The $\mu$-slice peaks at the sample mean and the $\sigma^2$-slice at the mean squared
deviation $\frac1n\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)^2$ — both readable straight off
@eq-loglik-normal-unknown by the same flatten-the-slope logic. Note the printed comparison:
the likelihood's variance peak uses divisor $n$, **not** the $n-1$ of the familiar "sample
variance." That is not a bug — the likelihood genuinely prefers the divisor-$n$ estimate,
and the resulting small bias is a famous and instructive feature of maximum likelihood that
[INFR-B2] dissects in full. We flag it here so the discrepancy does not surprise you; the
likelihood is being internally consistent, just not deferential to a convention invented for
a different reason.

## At the edge: when the likelihood alone is not enough {#sec-ex-edge}

The likelihood *ranks* parameters; it does not, by itself, tell you whether the best-ranked
parameter is any good, nor whether the model you read backwards was the right model to read
at all. Here is the honest boundary, in two parts.

**Part A — a flat likelihood: when the data barely discriminate.** With very little data, or
data that genuinely fail to separate competing parameters, the likelihood curve can be
nearly *flat* near its top. The peak still exists, but a wide band of parameters explains the
data almost equally well — the ranking is real but toothless. @fig-edge-flat contrasts a
sharp likelihood ($n=200$ flips) with a nearly flat one ($n=4$ flips): both peak at the same
$\hat p$, but only one of them *means* much.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge-flat
#| fig-cap: 'A peak is not the same as a *sharp* peak. Both coins landed on the same proportion of heads ($\hat p=0.75$), so both likelihoods peak at $p=0.75$ (green). With $n=200$ flips (red) the likelihood is a tight spike — the data pin the bias down. With $n=4$ flips (grey) it is a broad mound — a huge range of biases explains ''3 of 4 heads'' nearly as well. The location of the peak is identical; its *informativeness* is not. Quantifying this sharpness is exactly Fisher information ([INFR-B3]).'
p_grid = np.linspace(1e-3, 1 - 1e-3, 600)

def norm_loglik_curve(n, k):
    ell = k * np.log(p_grid) + (n - k) * np.log(1 - p_grid)
    return ell - ell.max()   # shift peak to 0 so shapes are comparable

ell_big = norm_loglik_curve(200, 150)
ell_small = norm_loglik_curve(4, 3)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(p_grid, ell_big, color=CURVE, label=r"$n=200$ (sharp)")
ax.plot(p_grid, ell_small, color=GREY, label=r"$n=4$ (flat)")
ax.axvline(0.75, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"shared peak $\hat p=0.75$")
ax.set_ylim(-8, 0.5)
ax.set_xlabel("bias $p$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"log-likelihood (peak shifted to 0)")
ax.set_title("Same peak, very different sharpness")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The likelihood was honest both times — it ranked the parameters correctly given the data. It
simply cannot manufacture certainty the data do not contain. The *width* of the peak, not its
location, carries that warning, and reading the width is what Fisher information ([INFR-B3])
and likelihood-based intervals ([INFR-C3]) are built to do. Hold the lesson: **the likelihood
gives you the ranking and the peak; it does not, on its own, give you uncertainty.**

**Part B — a likelihood for the wrong model.** The likelihood reads *whatever model you hand
it* backwards. If the model is wrong, the construction proceeds flawlessly and returns a
confident-looking peak that is meaningless. @fig-edge-misspec fits a Normal likelihood to
data that are actually heavy-tailed and skewed: the machinery dutifully reports a
most-explanatory $(\mu,\sigma^2)$, but the model never described the data.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge-misspec
#| fig-cap: 'The likelihood cannot tell you the model is wrong. The data (histogram) are right-skewed and heavy-tailed (a log-normal), but we read a *Normal* likelihood backwards anyway. It returns a perfectly definite peak $(\hat\mu,\hat\sigma^2)$ and the fitted Normal density (red) — which visibly misses the skew and the tail. The arithmetic is impeccable; the *answer* is not, because the model was misspecified. The likelihood ranks parameters **within** the model you chose; it never validates the choice.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
data = rng.lognormal(mean=1.0, sigma=0.6, size=300)   # skewed, heavy-tailed
mu_hat = data.mean()
v_hat = np.mean((data - mu_hat) ** 2)
sd_hat = np.sqrt(v_hat)

xs = np.linspace(0, data.max(), 400)
normal_fit = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - mu_hat) / sd_hat) ** 2) / (sd_hat * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(data, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.7, label="data (skewed)")
ax.plot(xs, normal_fit, color=CURVE,
        label=fr"fitted Normal $\mathcal{{N}}({mu_hat:.2f},\,{v_hat:.2f})$")
ax.axvline(mu_hat, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"likelihood peak $\hat\mu$")
ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("A confident peak for the wrong model")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A likelihood is only as good as the model you read backwards.** The construction in this
article assumes you have already *chosen* a probability model — Bernoulli, Poisson, Normal —
and it ranks parameter values *within that choice*. It has no opinion about whether the
choice was right. A Normal likelihood will happily hand you a most-explanatory mean and
variance for data that are skewed, multimodal, or heavy-tailed, and nothing in the
likelihood machinery raises an alarm. Checking the model itself — residual diagnostics,
goodness-of-fit tests, posterior predictive checks — is a separate discipline (the testing
side of this track, and the diagnostics side of Regression). Maximum likelihood gives you
the best parameter *given* a model; it never tells you the model was worth fitting.
:::

# When the likelihood is the right tool — and when it is not {#sec-applicability}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you build a likelihood, and when would you reach for something else?"* The
honest summary:

- **Reach for the likelihood** whenever you have a *parametric* probability model you
  believe describes the data-generating process — a named distribution (or a structured
  family like a regression model) with a finite set of unknown parameters. The likelihood is
  then the single most efficient summary of what the data say about those parameters: it
  underlies maximum likelihood ([INFR-B2]), the precision bounds of Fisher information
  ([INFR-B3]), likelihood-ratio tests ([INFR-D3]), and the Bayesian posterior ([INFR-E4]).
  Nearly all of classical and modern parametric inference is the likelihood in disguise.
- **Reach for something else** when (a) you are **unwilling to commit to a parametric
  model** — then nonparametric and resampling methods like the **bootstrap** ([INFR-E1])
  estimate uncertainty without a likelihood at all; (b) the model is **badly misspecified**,
  where, as @fig-edge-misspec shows, the likelihood is confidently wrong — robust or
  distribution-free methods are safer; (c) the likelihood is **intractable** — no closed
  form for the density (common in complex generative models), where approximate or
  simulation-based inference takes over; or (d) the data are **too few to discriminate**
  (@fig-edge-flat), where any single-number estimate, likelihood-based or not, is fragile and
  honest reporting must foreground the uncertainty.
- **The deepest caveat:** the likelihood ranks parameters *within* a model and says nothing
  about the model's correctness, and on its own it makes **no probability statement about the
  parameter**. Turning it into "how sure are we about $\theta$" requires either the
  frequentist sampling-distribution machinery (intervals and tests, the rest of this track)
  or a Bayesian prior ([INFR-E4]). The likelihood is the raw material of inference, not the
  finished statement.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. In code you almost never form
the raw product. You write the **log-likelihood** as a function of the parameters and a
sum over the data — `loglik(theta) = sum(dist.logpmf(x, theta))` or `.logpdf` for
continuous models — using the library's `logpmf`/`logpdf` methods, which are implemented to
be numerically stable (they compute the log directly rather than logging a tiny number, the
underflow lesson of @fig-underflow). `scipy.stats` exposes `.logpdf`/`.logpmf` for every
named distribution; `statsmodels` and most fitting libraries expose a `.loglike` or
`.score` method that returns exactly the $\ell(\theta)$ and the score $U(\theta)$ of this
article, and `.fit()` then maximizes the former by chasing the root of the latter — which is
precisely the program of [INFR-B2]. When you call `.fit()`, you now know the object it is
climbing and the slope it is following to the top.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*]**, which established that a
sample statistic is a random variable with a sampling distribution. There we *guessed* good
estimators; here we built the object that will *manufacture* them. From the **Likelihood
Spine** the trunk continues:

- **[INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*]** — turn the likelihood into a single best estimate by
  finding its peak: solve the score equation $U(\hat\theta)=0$, confirm it is a maximum,
  and derive the closed-form MLEs (Bernoulli $\hat p=k/n$, Poisson $\hat\lambda=\bar x$,
  Normal $\hat\mu=\bar x$ and $\hat\sigma^2=\frac1n\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2$) we previewed here as
  the roots of the score.
- **[INFR-B3 *Fisher Information*]** — read the *curvature* of $\ell$ at the peak (the
  variance of the score from @sec-score), which is exactly the "sharpness" we saw vary in
  @fig-edge-flat, and turn it into a hard lower bound on any estimator's variance.
- **[INFR-B4 *Why Maximum Likelihood Works*]** — the asymptotics: as data accumulate the
  log-likelihood's peak homes in on the truth and its shape becomes Gaussian.
- **[INFR-E4 *The Bayesian Turn*]** — the likelihood of this article, *multiplied by a
  prior* and re-normalized, becomes the posterior. The Bayesian fork is where the forbidden
  phrase "the probability that $\theta = 0.7$" finally becomes legitimate — and you will see
  exactly what extra ingredient (the prior) had to be added to license it.

It also feeds outward: the Gaussian-likelihood / least-squares equivalence of
@sec-normal-known is the bridge to the Regression track's [REGR-01], where the same identity
arrives from the geometry of fitting a line.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding source.** R. A. Fisher, "On the Mathematical Foundations of Theoretical
  Statistics," *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A* 222 (1922) — where the
  word *likelihood* is coined and the distinction from probability is drawn and defended.
  Fisher returned to the idea repeatedly; his 1925 *Statistical Methods for Research Workers*
  put it to work.
- **The likelihood principle.** A. Birnbaum, "On the Foundations of Statistical Inference,"
  *JASA* 57 (1962): the claim that *all* the evidence in the data about $\theta$ is carried
  by the likelihood function — a deep, contested thesis that explains why the likelihood
  recurs everywhere, and why dropping its constant (as we did freely) is legitimate.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How exactly do we find the peak, and
  is it always unique?* — maximum likelihood, [INFR-B2], including a likelihood with no
  maximum (the uniform $[0,\theta]$) where the score approach fails outright. *How sharp is
  the peak, and what does that buy?* — Fisher information and the Cramér–Rao bound,
  [INFR-B3]. *What is the sampling distribution of the peak's location?* — the asymptotic
  normality of the MLE, [INFR-B4]. *When is "the probability of $\theta$" a legal sentence?*
  — only after a prior, [INFR-E4].
